title: Arbitrary parallel entangling gates with independent calibration on a trapped ion quantum computer
authors: Matthew Diaz, Masoud Mohammadi-Arzanagh, Yingyue Zhu, Mohammad Hafezi, Norbert M. Linke, Alaina M. Green, Arthur Y. Nam
abstract: Parallel processing of information plays a critical role in accelerating computation. This includes quantum computers, where parallel processing of quantum information will play a critical role in practical quantum advantage. Here, we demonstrate a new type of parallel entangling gates in a trapped-ion quantum computer, that simultaneously provides efficient gate-pulse synthesis and calibration, as well as graph-pattern-agnostic implementation. We demonstrate the resulting reduced execution time in three well-known algorithms, exhibiting disjoint gates, a star graph and a ring graph respectively. For disjoint qubit pairs the execution time of our parallel gates is comparable to that of a single-pair entangling gate resulting in an approximately linear speed up. For all graph patterns our parallel gate fidelities are comparable to the fidelity of a single-pair entangling gate. These advantages motivate architectures featuring multiple medium length ion chains in future quantum computing devices.
summary: This paper presents a new method for implementing arbitrary parallel entangling gates in a trapped-ion quantum computer. By using an iterative pulse-shaping technique, the authors can execute multiple gates simultaneously across different graph patterns without needing unique calibrations for every possible combination. The approach significantly reduces execution time for various quantum algorithms while maintaining high gate fidelities. This advancement is crucial for scaling quantum architectures, particularly for multi-chain setups.
main_problem: The challenge of accelerating quantum computation in trapped-ion systems by implementing arbitrary parallel entangling gates without incurring exponential increases in power, gate time, or control complexity.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a pulse-synthesis method that enables graph-pattern-agnostic parallel gates with fidelities comparable to single-pair gates, achieving significant algorithmic speedups in tasks like the Heisenberg Hamiltonian simulation.
method: An iterative three-step pulse synthesis approach using sine-based basis functions, a maximum weight matching problem for mode assignment, and a projector operator to explicitly cancel qubit-qubit crosstalk.
model_or_system: A trapped-ion quantum computer using 171Yb+ ions, where entanglement is driven via Mølmer-Sørensen (XX) gates through spin-motion coupling in the Lamb-Dicke regime.
key_observables: Gate fidelity (via parity scans), entanglement phase angle, pulse power (RMS Rabi frequency), and gate duration.
important_parameters: Lamb-Dicke parameters, motional mode frequencies, gate duration, and the number of ions (N).
main_assumptions: Assumes off-resonant coupling to other modes is naturally suppressed and that accurate estimation of Lamb-Dicke parameters is possible.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows calculated pulse functions; Figure 4 compares output state probabilities for HS and BV algorithms; Figure 5 shows magnetization evolution in HH simulations; Figure 6 compares power scaling and power-time tradeoffs; Figure 7 shows power requirements vs. gate duration for various ion chain lengths.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of parallelization, describes the physical platform and Hamiltonian, details the iterative pulse synthesis and power rebalancing methods, presents experimental/simulated benchmarks for various algorithms (HS, BV, HH), and discusses scaling and hardware implications.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum optics and AMO because it provides a scalable control protocol for many-body dynamics in ion chains, addressing the fundamental trade-off between gate speed, power, and crosstalk in driven quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
